item.content:encoded:
If there's one skip that I've pondered deleting the most over my 2.5 years on this site, it's this one. The fact that it hit +100 is proof that HTML format screw trumps writing, and I don't like it being an example of that on the site.

Does anyone have any good ideas for a rewrite? It's been well over 2 years since the initial context that led to this skip being a thing, and I'm still trying to figure out how to redo this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this skip, the format screw is an excellent way of grabbing a readers attention, but I have a few critiques. Most of my bugbears have already been brought up, but I think the main problem is that the skip begins to collapse under it's own weight after a while. There's simply too much intertwined that doesn't really pay off to it's maximum potential. The GOI stuff feels super unneeded.

Still upvoting, but very close margin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The formatting for this is fantastic and I absolutely loved it, well done! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no voting, instead of upvoting for 2 reasons.

1. Towards the end, it starts losing steam (Just like Bleach, am I right?), and while the ending could've been interesting, I was too worn out. This could be because I've read 2 other Black Hole SCPs, and so when it throws black holes in there, it just doesn't seem interesting, except for the robots that destroy them.

2. This is more of a personal thing, but there's too many crosslinks, which I need to read to understand parts of it, and then on some of those there are other crosslinks, and then it takes about an hour to read through the scp. Though that's moreso my fault than yours.

I'm not downvoting, however, because there are some legitamently good parts in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the gif in the beginning has "result in" twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So interesting. However, I think that this article need not for GAW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Binary isn't a language, it's a number system. If the recipient can decode the message as it stands, there is no reason to establish a common number system with prime numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The format screw was actually done greatly, but the suspension of disbelief constantly being broken and the sheer scale of absurdity that this anomaly goes to makes this wasted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't downvote this, despite the dialogue that I, personally, thought was lacking. The science analysis was genuinely interesting to read, and the formatting is insanely well done. Good job, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the presentation was clean by the end of the article, but I realized I had no idea what was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
psul has taken the words out of my mouth/thumbs.

The main things that let down the piece for me were the overcomplicated, blandly explained reveal, and that I don't have enough affection for the Xia and GAW GoIs to enjoy their appearance in a scip.

The formatting is really neat though. You should be proud of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it: a nitpick. Would add Binary to the list of languages used. Or something like "A list of prime numbers in binary is then played." Kurgezast is awesome btw. Also, the footnote 1. doesn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am down-voting - but only just - and here's why:

  • as ARD says, this is *very* similar to 1739 and a lot of things that this does (even including the coat analogy vs the dog and ball analogy) it does less well than that skip;
  • this has several tropes - dangerous anomaly hiding behind innocent anomaly, increasing clearance revealing changing object classes - that are pretty old-hat now;
  • the animated text is unnecessarily flashy - I'd rather enjoy the steak than the sizzle
  • I found the cross-links incredibly distracting - there didn't seem any need to link this into either GAW or the Xia, and the eventual story (true vacuum caused by an entity, which was captured by a space machine built by the Xia, escapes but somehow(??) gets stuck in the Foundation database, but is then let out accidentally by GAW trying to hack the Foundation) is too convoluted for my suspension of disbelief;
  • why do the Xia describe the actions of their machine in modern English (Document 3747-34)?
  • why is level 5 access not allowed to view Document 3747-34 when lower clearances are? or does this anticipate that level 5 staff are required to read multiple levels of documentation to understand an anomaly, which seems inefficient?
  • it's never made clear how breaking the computer saves the other universe. SCP-3747-B-K already exists in the other universe, and breaking the computer just stops new false vacuums being spawned, according to Graham 2 (not to mention that everything in the other universe - names, passcodes, entire personal histories, the numeric designation of this anomaly - is exactly the same as our universe, except for the true/false vacuum thing, and the blackboxed numeric designation of one other random skip? unless that's intended to be fridge horror?);
  • it's also not clear why breaking the computer doesn't release the evil entity (which is trapped there) rather than killing it;
  • crucially, the Level 1 and Level 2 versions of the article refer to SCP-3747-A without then explaining what it is. That is either a terribly unhelpful database entry, or a huge failure of InfoSec.

In its defense, this used the iteration format to very good effect, subverting the standard constant escalation and keeping the reader on their toes as to what was really going on. And the writing was good throughout. But it all kinda falls under its own weight by the end, it makes less sense the more you think about it, and 1739 did most of this better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GAW being able to casually hack the Foundation database is, yeah, kind of ridiculous, but I was able to accept it while I read, so I don't think it was a problem.

I really like the use of increasing clearance levels to reveal more of the story. And it isn't just "here's more info every time"; the major change in that last one moves the story ahead, and there's still expungement! I mean, I've seen it before, but this is an example of it used well, and I'll really never get tired of it. A few things:

  • We don't use electrons to make anything. That wording caught me off guard.
  • In the interview, Graham 2 says "you were in the lead" twice.
  • What's with "apon"? The first looked like a typo, the second makes me think it's indicative of something, but I can't figure out what.
  • And there's a big void at the end of clearance 5 that doesn't seem to exist for any purpose. Not sure what could be done about that.

In all, a pretty good skip with cool execution, nicely done. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, you posted before I could critique :p.

I agree with ARD about how GAW is characterised here, but otherwise, I think this is excellent. It's simultaneously apocalyptic and also low-key, which is excellent. It riffs off 2317's structure a bit, but it works well despite that and manages to differentiate itself nicely. Good job, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon reading this article, I am struck almost immediately by how closely it resembles SCP-1739, from a Dell laptop catalyzing the destruction of another universe to a researcher talking to his alternate counterpart . Unfortunately, the conversation between David 1 and David A is far less compelling, as is the general anomaly.

Furthermore, the SCP-2317-esque escalation fails to grab me, though the subversion of the final object class is commendable. The connection to Gamers Against Weed is unnecessary at best and suspension-breakingly absurd at worst. Finally, the animated text does absolutely nothing to enhance the reading - the fact that you kept the debugging skip tool shows that it actually hinders it.

The effort put into the presentation is admirable; the story being told, however, is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Pecan, BenjaminStokes, bittermixin, The Great Hippo, Rikks and many others for helping me with this skip through its many stages. I stole most of the formatting from qntm's SCP-3125, and MatthewMueller from JSFiddle for the typewriting code.

Protip for re-readers: if you click on the warning, the collapsible shows up immediately. If you click on the "verifying personnel clearance" thingamobobber, the selector shows up immediately. This was the only thing keeping me sane while debugging this bad boy.

Inspiration

Edit: I'd like to bring attention to a Japanese translation by EFU and a Chinese translation by Rakish_Rambler.
